body:
# Feature request

Would be great having button component as well, now you can tell ok u can do it by yourself, but buttons have a tons of aria ttributes and it's very useful everywhere.. 😁💪

## Summary

https://intentui.com/docs/components/buttons/button

## Examples in other libraries

https://intentui.com/docs/components/buttons/button

## Motivation

Most useful component, used everywhere

comments.body:
> buttons have a tons of aria attributes

@vitalijalbu what do you mean? which aria attributes?
See https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/2138 for why we don't have this and what you could do instead in your project @vitalijalbu

> See [#2138](https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/2138) for why we don't have this and what you could do instead in your project [@vitalijalbu](https://github.com/vitalijalbu)

yeah but guys, it's not only a simple button, you have many variants of that:

- icon start
- icon end
- loading state
- disabled state

and many others props...

All ui librararies does it... I don't understand why you don't want to add it...

Base UI is a headless library, so icon stuff is out of scope (you already control the styles)

For loading/disabled states, isn't that straightforward to add yourself?

If the library would add a Button component, it would be to implement `focusableWhenDisabled` + `Enter`/`Space` functionality when rendering a non-native-`<button>` tag - but aside from that, there isn't a reason for an unstyled library to have a button component

> For loading/disabled states, isn't that straightforward to add yourself?

Implementing a loading/pending state is [not so straightforward](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/Button.html#pending), so offering a Button component could be interesting in that regard. I see it as a similar case as Avatar: it's not "needed" but it's nice to have.

> Implementing a loading/pending state is [not so straightforward](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/Button.html#pending)

As far as I can tell, this is just setting `aria-disabled="true"` on the `<button>` (i.e. the `focusableWhenDisabled` behavior); it doesn't do anything special necessarily

The `usePress` logic would affect the components that use `useButton` internally if we were to normalize some of these behaviors: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/1726

- This could be useful via the Base UI parts API sure - from that link, it just seemed like you could use the `isPending` state from the outside in the react-aria example to conditionally render the `Progress` component, instead of needing `children` render prop(?)
- In that example, the only special ARIA attr is `aria-disabled`, and then rendering a `Progress` component inside with `aria-label`

I didn't mean to use exact same implementation from RAC, it was just an example to showcase that it might not be as straightforward to implement correctly. I wouldn't rely on users conditionally rendering the loading indicator in an hypothetical Base UI API.

I've tentatively added `Button` to our unresolved Notion doc to resolve for July. 

The `Avatar` comparison imo is a valid argument for why we should support `Button` that handles `focusableWhenDisabled` + `pending` state in an accessible way (since the `disabled` attribute isn't accessible to convey a loading state)

very agree guys, headless doesn't mean have only a few components... or we don't do it cause other libraries (like radix or ark ui) doesn't have a button, let's be better.

See react-aria-components (my favorite one, and I adopted it because of these problems)... it has everything you need!
See at intent ui: https://intentui.com/docs/components/buttons/button.

@vitalijalbu The goal of an unstyled library is to provide low-level web primitives, where HTML elements are not sufficient. That might be because a) the HTML elements are missing functionality (e.g. `<input type="range" />` missing shift + right/left), b) because the provided HTML primitive can't be styled properly (e.g. `<select>`) , or c) because a good HTML primitive doesn't exist (e.g. Combobox etc.).

These concerns don't exist for `<button>`. Generally speaking, you can just apply CSS to `<button>` and you're good to go. You can just apply the `disabled` attribute, and that works too. There's nothing for us to do here.

Now, you could add a fourth qualifier to the mix: d) where the provided HTML primitive has a bunch of browser bugs + inconsistencies that we can patch. These issues do exist with `<button>`, but it wouldn't take priority over important new primitives we need to build.

But in any case, highly-opinionated, high-level APIs such as `iconStart`, `iconEnd` would be outside the scope of any headless lib. No headless lib provides these APIs, because they're super opinionated, and developers want different APIs here.

To put it simply, the idea is that Base UI provides JS + a11y, and you provide the styles + high-level APIs.

I see two rationales for why we might want to make a button primitive like this public that were not mentioned in this thread: 

1. **Supporting a larger diversity of DOM nodes as button children.** https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/9705#issuecomment-2661343332, in short, a `<button>` element only accept *phrasing elements* which prevents rendering elements like h1, div as children. This leads to challenges like #2520. For a developer, the simplest would be to do `<Button render={<div>}>`.
2. **Supporting links that look like a button.** The developer has a design system, he creates a button component. However, his designers built a UI where the element looks like a button but behaves like a link (render a `<a>` not a `<button>`). We will see this come up with Joy UI. The developer is left with two choices: create a new component for that use case, duplicate the button CSS to apply it to a link, or update their button component to support `render={<a>}`. If Base UI had a component here, I guess it would simplify the work.

And the ones mentioned previously:

3. **Loading state**. https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/2225#issuecomment-3027925754
Can I help with this in any way?

@battaglr the PR implementing this component was `on hold` as there was some confusion around the props it should accept. 

I've refactored it in #2363 so it's ready for review again

This feature will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui-components/react@2363
```

> 2\. **Supporting links that look like a button.** The developer has a design system, he creates a button component. However, his designers built a UI where the element looks like a button but behaves like a link (render a `<a>` not a `<button>`). We will see this come up with Joy UI. The developer is left with two choices: create a new component for that use case, duplicate the button CSS to apply it to a link, or update their button component to support `render={<a>}`. If Base UI had a component here, I guess it would simplify the work.

There's another choice that I don't see mentioned here, which is to abstract the button as a set of classes that can be applied to any element. This decouples the styles from the element—not everything should be abstracted as a component.

> 2. **Supporting links that look like a button**. The developer has a design system, he creates a button component. However, his designers built a UI where the element looks like a button but behaves like a link (render a <a> not a <button>). We will see this come up with Joy UI. The developer is left with two choices: create a new component for that use case, duplicate the button CSS to apply it to a link, or update their button component to support render={<a>}. If Base UI had a component here, I guess it would simplify the work.

I brought this up in the Discord channel just a couple of days ago. From what I understand, the team isn't inclined to support `render={<a>}`, which is perfectly reasonable since a `Button` component should return a button, not a link.

That said, aside from keeping a design system built on the top of Base UI simpler (we'd avoid introducing a new component for this use case only) it would also feel more familiar to people coming from shadcn/Radix.

I am happy to adapt to whatever direction we choose, just wondering when we might have a final decision on this.

labels.name: component: button
labels.description: Changes related to the button component.

labels.name: type: new feature

title: [button] Button component
